title: Type 1 Diabetes -- Treatment / Management -- Hypoglycemia
content: Patients must be educated about the signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia, the blood glucose thresholds for treatment, and the use of glucagon for severe hypoglycemia unresponsive to conventional treatments. Individuals at risk for recurrent and severe hypoglycemia benefit from using a CGM, which can alert them to treat hypoglycemia promptly. Hypoglycemia treatment generally involves consuming 15 to 20 grams of glucose orally if blood glucose levels fall below 70 mg/dL. Blood glucose should be rechecked 15 minutes later, with additional carbohydrates administered if necessary. Once blood glucose levels have normalized, a snack should be provided to prevent recurrence. Glucagon should be prescribed for emergency use in cases of severe hypoglycemia when the individual cannot consume carbohydrates orally.

title: Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme Inhibitors (ACEI) -- Indications -- Diabetes
content: The Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone system and increased glomerular capillary pressure have been reported to increase the progression of renal dysfunction due to diabetes mellitus related nephropathy. [22] A large, prospective, randomized, placebo-controlled has demonstrated that ACE inhibitors slow down the progression of nephropathy in patients with insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus and significantly reduce the combined endpoints of dialysis, transplantation, and death. [23] Current recommendations are using ACEi or ARB as first-line therapy for hypertension in patients with a history of diabetes. Also, the use of ACEi in diabetic hypertensive patients with no history of coronary heart disease has been shown to decrease the incidence of myocardial infarction and improve heart function. [24]

title: Aspart Insulin -- Monitoring
content: Critically ill patients receiving insulin aspart should have their blood glucose level tested every 1 to 2 hours. Non-critically ill patients on insulin aspart should routinely monitor their blood glucose concentration at home or in the hospital to assess the efficacy of the insulin dose. This testing ideally occurs before or 2 hours after a meal. Insulin dose adjustments should be based on testing results, typically 10% to 20% in either direction. All patients on insulin therapy should have their HgbA1c tested every 6 months and electrolyte levels tested annually. Hemoglobin A1c should be monitored quarterly in patients who do not meet treatment goals or after changes in therapy. According to the American Diabetes Association, monitoring goals include a fasting blood glucose level of 80 to 130 mg/dL, a peak postprandial (1 to 2 hours post-meal) blood glucose level of less than 180 mg/dL, and a HgbA1c level less than 7.0% for non-pregnant adult patients. These goals may change for individual patients based on age, duration of diabetes, comorbid conditions, hypoglycemia unawareness, risk of a hypoglycemic event, and other considerations. Patients with more comorbid conditions or a higher risk of harm during a hypoglycemic event have less stringent goals. According to the Endocrine Society guidelines, patients with type 1 diabetes who receive multiple daily injections should receive continuous glucose monitoring instead of self-monitoring via fingerstick. Continuous glucose monitoring should be initiated in the inpatient setting for patients who are at high risk for hypoglycemia. [7]

title: Type 1 Diabetes -- Treatment / Management -- Hypoglycemia
content: Hypoglycemia is the most frequent adverse effect of insulin therapy. Educating people living with T1D and their partners about the signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia is crucial. Symptoms include sweating, rapid heartbeat, lightheadedness, confusion, hunger, visual changes, and tremors. Symptoms of hypoglycemia typically occur when blood glucose levels drop below 70 mg/dL, known as level 1 hypoglycemia. Level 2 hypoglycemia occurs when blood glucose levels fall below 54 mg/dL and is usually associated with cognitive impairment and altered consciousness. Level 3 hypoglycemia involves a hypoglycemic episode that requires assistance from another person for resuscitation.

title: Type 1 Diabetes -- Treatment / Management -- Hypoglycemia
content: Recurrent hypoglycemic episodes, particularly in individuals with a longer duration of T1D, can lead to hypoglycemia unawareness. This condition occurs when individuals experience hypoglycemia symptoms at progressively lower glucose thresholds, likely due to reduced sympathoadrenal responses or autonomic failure. Hypoglycemia not only impacts the quality of life in people living with T1D but also increases the risk of cardiovascular events and mortality. In older adults or frail individuals, hypoglycemia can heighten the risk of falls, cognitive impairment, dementia, and fractures.

title: ACE Inhibitors -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
content: ACE inhibitors may delay the progression of nephropathy and reduce the risks of cardiovascular events in hypertensive patients with diabetes mellitus type I and type II. The addition of an ACE inhibitor has shown a significant reduction in serum creatinine in hypertensive and normotensive patients with albuminuria. ACE inhibitor therapy has shown a significant decrease in the progression of diabetic nephropathy. [10] [11] Krecak I. et al. found evidence that ACE inhibitors might have renoprotective properties in patients with polycythemia vera. [12] Yao J.et al. discovered that ACE inhibitors improve insulin sensitivity in hypertensive patients with diabetes mellitus compared to angiotensin II receptor blockers. [13] Salmenkari H. et al. found evidence that the use of ACE inhibitors and angiotensin II receptor blockers in patients with inflammatory bowel disease results in fewer hospitalizations, glucocorticoid use, and a milder disease course. [14] The treatment with ACE inhibitors in adult patients with the diagnosis of nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), now also called metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), especially those with associated chronic kidney disease, is associated with decreased risk of liver-related events (LRE), defined as the risk of cirrhosis complications and liver cancer. [15] [16] Hypertensive patients with Alzheimer's disease (AD) treated with ACE inhibitors demonstrated intact physical capacity, improved hand grip strength, and prevention of neuromuscular junction degradation, as indicated by the maintenance of plasma C-terminal agrin fragment-22 (CAF22) levels. [17] Similar findings of better hand grip strength and gait speed, but low CAF22 levels, were also noted in CHF patients treated with ACE inhibitors. [18] ACE inhibitors are also used in scleroderma renal crisis. [19] [20] A recent study found that renin-angiotensin system (RAS) blockers, including ACE inhibitors, provided similar mortality benefits in both Black and non-Black patients with heart failure and reduced ejection fraction. Although the relative reduction in heart failure hospitalizations was smaller in Black patients, the absolute benefit was comparable due to their higher baseline risk. [21] The meta-analysis suggests that renin-angiotensin system inhibitors, including ACE inhibitors, are associated with a reduced mortality rate in patients with atrial fibrillation; however, further large-scale trials are needed to confirm these findings. [22]

title: Aspart Insulin -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
content: Antidiabetic agents, ACE inhibitors, fluoxetine, fibrates, monoamine oxidase inhibitors, salicylates, and octreotide can increase the risk of hypoglycemia. When using insulin aspart in conjunction with these medications, increasing the frequency of glucose monitoring is advised. Drugs that may reduce the blood glucose-lowering effect of insulin aspart include corticosteroids, diuretics, estrogens, niacin, oral contraceptives, protease inhibitors, thyroid hormone, albuterol, epinephrine, and atypical antipsychotics (eg, olanzapine, clozapine). [20] Dose adjustment of insulin and more frequent glucose monitoring may be required. Pentamidine may increase or decrease the blood glucose-reducing effect of insulin aspart. When insulin aspart is concomitantly administered with pentamidine, dose adjustments may be necessary. Beta-blockers and clonidine may mask the signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia and should be used with caution. [21] [22]

title: Antihypertensive Medications -- Indications -- ACE Inhibitors and ARBs
content: Ramipril has been shown to decrease mortality, the incidence of stroke, and MIs when used in patients with symptomatic heart failure or asymptomatic patients with low ejection fraction. The research found perindopril decreases cardiovascular events when used in a patient with stable coronary artery disease and normal systolic dysfunction. Compared with atenolol, losartan was found to be better in reducing morbidity and mortality and better in lowering blood pressure. Comparing ramipril with telmisartan, they were equivalent in effect in diabetic or heart failure patients, with telmisartan showing a correlation with less angioedema. [14]

title: Type 1 Diabetes -- Evaluation
content: Evaluating glycemic control by checking HbA1c levels is recommended every 3 months during each follow-up visit. Other laboratory tests that should be conducted, if not performed within the past year, include a lipid profile, serum creatinine, spot urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio, liver function tests, thyroid-stimulating hormone, complete blood count with platelets, and serum potassium—especially if the patient is also taking an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor, angiotensin receptor blocker, or diuretic. Since people with T1D are at an increased risk of developing other autoimmune diseases, such as autoimmune thyroid conditions, celiac disease, primary adrenal insufficiency, and rheumatoid arthritis, screening for autoimmune disorders should be considered when clinically appropriate. [18]

title: Somogyi Phenomenon -- Clinical Significance -- Hypoglycemia vs. Hyperglycemia
content: Hyperglycemic episodes are also to be avoided. Symptoms to look out for include polyuria, polydipsia, weakness, tachycardia, and hypotension. [11] The hyperglycemia will worsen the cytokine and inflammatory response, causing even more hyperglycemia. [11] Consequences of hyperglycemia include renal failure, polyneuropathy, retinopathy, and arrhythmias. [11] Hyperglycemia produces an increase in advanced glycation end products (AGEs), which deposit in the body, causing connective tissue crosslinking, fibrosis, and impaired relaxation of the heart. [12] People with diabetes become insulin-resistant, which keeps blood glucose levels elevated, causing an even more increase in AGEs. [12] With the failure to keep blood glucose levels at a normal range, the hyperglycemic episodes also induce the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS), increasing vascular resistance and arterial pressure in the body. [12]

title: Captopril -- Indications -- Diabetic Nephropathy
content: Treatment of diabetic nephropathy (proteinuria >500 mg/d) in patients with type 1 insulin-dependent diabetes and retinopathy. An angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, captopril, is FDA-approved to treat diabetic nephropathy. Enalapril is not FDA-approved to treat diabetic nephropathy. [3] Captopril decreases the rate of progression of renal insufficiency by controlling blood pressure. With chronic renal failure, ACE inhibitors or ARBs must be dose-adjusted for patients with renal impairment. [1]

title: Type 1 Diabetes (Nursing) -- Medical Management
content: All patients with T1DM require insulin therapy. Multiple daily insulin injections (MDI) using a basal/bolus insulin regimen or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion through an insulin pump are the preferred treatment. The patient’s weight in kilograms is multiplied by 0.5 to 0.6 units to calculate the initial total daily insulin dose (TDD) in an adult. In general, 40% to 50% of the total daily dose comprises the patient’s long-acting insulin needs. The other half approximates the daily short-acting insulin that needs to be given before or with meals. Dosing is modified based on many factors, including diet and physical activity. Adjustments can also be made based on self-monitoring of blood glucose or continuous glucose monitoring results. When possible, patients should be taught carbohydrate counting and instructed to use an insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio that approximates the grams of carbohydrates that will be covered by one unit of insulin. A carbohydrate-consistent diet is helpful if carbohydrate counting is not realistic due to poor numeracy or other reasons. Patients should be taught which foods contain carbohydrates and benefit from meeting with a dietician. It is also recommended to calculate a correction factor, which can be initially estimated using the formula 1800 divided by the TDD. This estimates the fall in blood glucose for every 1 unit of insulin given. This number will need to be adjusted per subsequent glucose monitoring results. [11] [12]

title: New Onset Diabetes After Transplant -- Treatment / Management
content: Because patients with transplants are at increased risk of hypoglycemia and frequently have a concurrent history of heart disease, guidelines recommend an HbA1c goal of 7 to 7.5% in these patients. Studies demonstrated that in patients with hypertension and congestive heart failure, using ACEIs or ARBs has a significant role in reducing the incidence of NODAT. Treatment of infections such as HCV should be a consideration before transplant. [20] [18] [8] [7] [12]

title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1100
content: The human insulin receptor (INSR protein, human) is the molecular target of insulin aspart (human), a rapid‑acting insulin analogue. Insulin aspart is indicated for the treatment of both type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1) and type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2), as well as for diabetes without complications (مرض السكري دون مضاعفات). However, insulin aspart is contraindicated in the presence of hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر), a condition that belongs to the broader group of other endocrine disorders (other endocrine disorders). Diseases of the endocrine pancreas including diabetes (DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES) is classified as a subset of other endocrine disorders and encompasses several specific entities: type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, diabetes without complications, hypoglycemia, hyperinsulinemia (فرط أنسولين الدم), gastrin‑secretion disorder (اضطراب إفراز الجسترين), and glucagonoma (ورم غلوكاغوني). The gastrin‑secretion disorder further leads to gastric mucosal atrophy and associated secretion abnormalities (الحثل واضطرابات الإفراز في الغشاء المخاطي المَعِدِيّ المِعَوِيّ). Understanding these hierarchical relationships helps clinicians recognize how insulin aspart interacts with its receptor, its therapeutic role across different forms of diabetes, and the importance of avoiding its use in hypoglycemic states, while also situating diabetes within the wider spectrum of endocrine pancreatic diseases and related hormonal disorders.

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a classification that encompasses several distinct disorders of pancreatic endocrine function. It includes مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which denotes diabetes mellitus without any associated complications and represents the basic metabolic disturbance of chronic hyperglycemia. It also comprises اعتلال عصبي سكري, a chronic diabetic neuropathy in which prolonged hyperglycemia damages peripheral nerves, leading to sensory loss, pain, and autonomic dysfunction. The category further lists ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي, a progressive atrophy of one side of the facial tissues that can be observed in some patients with endocrine pancreatic disease. فرط أنسولين الدم is another child condition, describing an excess of circulating insulin that may arise from insulin‑producing tumors (insulinoma) or other dysregulation of pancreatic β‑cell activity. Finally, ورم غلوكاغوني refers to a glucagon‑secreting pancreatic tumor (glucagonoma) that causes hyperglycemia and a characteristic clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the full spectrum of pancreatic endocrine disorders—from uncomplicated diabetes to its neuropathic complications, hormonal excess states, and neoplastic growths—underscoring the need for comprehensive evaluation and targeted management in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 38
content: The therapeutic category “drugs used in diabetes” comprises insulin‑based products (insulins and analogues) and non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents. Within the insulin family, long‑acting injectable analogues such as insulin glargine, insulin degludec and combination formulations that pair a basal insulin with a rapid‑acting component (e.g., beef insulin and human insulin) are classified as long‑acting or intermediate/long‑acting plus fast‑acting insulins. Fixed‑dose combinations that couple a basal insulin with a glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue—insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide—provide complementary glycaemic control. The GLP‑1 analogue class includes exenatid, liraglutide and lixisenatide. Non‑insulin agents include alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, dipeptidyl peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors (e.g., alogliptin, sitagliptin) and biguanides (e.g., fenformin/metformin). Sitagliptin is incorporated in a fixed‑dose tablet with the statin simvastatin; simvastatin is contraindicated when co‑administered with the antiretroviral nelfinavir mesylate. This taxonomy links basal long‑acting insulin analogues, oral biguanides, DPP‑4 inhibitors and GLP‑1 analogues, highlighting their distinct mechanisms, common combination products, and important drug‑interaction considerations in diabetes management.

title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
content: The pharmacotherapy of diabetes is organized into two principal subclasses: insulins and analogues, and non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering agents. Insulins and analogues for injection used in diabetes comprise intermediate‑ or long‑acting formulations that may be combined with fast‑acting agents; this includes the long‑acting subgroup (e.g., insulin glargine, insulin degludec / liraglutide, insulin glargine / lixisenatide) and the intermediate/long‑acting combined with fast‑acting subgroup (e.g., insulin aspart, human, which targets the human INSR protein). Insulin, Beef is classified as a protein within this category. Non‑insulin agents are divided mechanistically into alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, dipeptidyl peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors, glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues, and biguanides. DPP‑4 inhibitors include alogliptin and sitagliptin; sitagliptin is also present in the fixed‑dose combination simvastatin / sitagliptin, which pairs the statin simvastatin with the DPP‑4 inhibitor. GLP‑1 analogues such as liraglutide and lixisenatide are incorporated into combination products with long‑acting insulins (insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide) to enhance glycaemic control. Biguanides, represented by phenformin and metformin, lower glucose by reducing hepatic production and increasing peripheral insulin sensitivity. Simvastatin, a lipid‑lowering agent, is contraindicated with cyclosporin and nelfinavir mesylate, highlighting important drug‑interaction considerations. Together, these hierarchical relationships and specific drug‑target interactions guide clinicians in selecting appropriate insulin regimens, oral agents, and combination therapies for safe and effective diabetes management.

title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1756
content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of disorders and clinical manifestations that arise from dysfunction of the pancreatic endocrine system. It includes “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia), which represents elevated blood‑glucose levels and is a primary feature of diabetes. “غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري” (diabetic hyperosmolar coma) is a severe, acute complication of uncontrolled hyperglycemia characterized by extreme dehydration and altered mental status. “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes mellitus without complications) denotes diabetes in its uncomplicated form, serving as the baseline condition from which other sequelae may develop. Among the chronic complications, “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy) reflects nerve damage caused by prolonged hyperglycemia, leading to sensory and motor deficits. “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive hemifacial atrophy) is listed as a possible extra‑pancreatic manifestation that can be associated with underlying endocrine pancreatic disease. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), an excess of circulating insulin that may precede or accompany insulin resistance. Finally, “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) represents a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, producing a distinct clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse acute, chronic, and neoplastic conditions linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of both glycemic control and associated complications.
